title: saveState close() error swallows original sync() error
description: The durability sequence uses try{ sync } finally { close }. If handle.sync() rejects (the load-bearing durability op), the await handle.close() in the finally block runs unconditionally and, if it also rejects (e.g. EBADF after a sync error invalidated the handle), the close() error replaces the sync() error. The caller sees the close error and never learns the durability flush failed.
impact: On real disk pressure (ENOSPC, I/O error during sync), the diagnostic path surfaces a misleading 'close failed' error instead of the actual sync failure. Cycle-2's durability invariant claims the canonical state was either fully synced or absent — when this code path fires it can leave the tmp file on disk while reporting a confusing close-time error, and the operator can't tell which operation actually failed.
recommendation: Capture the sync error explicitly: let syncErr; try { await handle.sync(); } catch (e) { syncErr = e; } finally { try { await handle.close(); } catch (closeErr) { if (!syncErr) syncErr = closeErr; } } if (syncErr) throw syncErr;

title: saveState leaks tmp file when rename or sync fails
description: writeFile(tmpPath) → open → sync → close → rename. If sync(), close(), or rename() rejects, the tmp file `state.tmp-<ts>-<rand>.json` remains in `.sui-deepbook-course/`. There is no unlink-on-failure cleanup. The wx flag on writeFile prevents collisions per call (random suffix), but every failed save accumulates an orphan tmp file.
impact: Repeated transient saveState failures (e.g. flaky disk, antivirus quarantine on Windows, cross-device EXDEV on rename) silently litter the state directory. On a constrained filesystem this can fill the .sui-deepbook-course/ directory with orphans the user never sees. Also leaks the sensitive 0o600 state contents in tmp form indefinitely.
recommendation: Wrap the open→sync→close→rename block in try/catch and unlink(tmpPath).catch(()=>{}) on any failure before re-throwing, so tmp files don't accumulate on persistent errors.

title: verify.ts global mutable _verifyOverride re-introduces the M005 anti-pattern A13 just retired
description: Cycle 4 A13 explicitly retires preflight.ts's setSpawnOverride / module-level spawnOverrides Map as the 'global-mutable test seam' anti-pattern (cycle-3 carry-forward M005/R2-003). verify.ts then introduces an identical pattern: a module-level `let _verifyOverride` plus an exported `setVerifyOverride` setter, used by the harness's withVerifyStub fixture. The cleanup callback only resets if the still-current override matches (`if (_verifyOverride === fn)`), so a second concurrent test that installs its own stub before the first cleanup runs will leave the original override leaked across tests. There is no try/finally guarantee at the harness level either — if a test throws between install and cleanup, the override persists into the next test.
impact: Test isolation breaks: a verify stub installed by one test can bleed into the next test if the test body throws or the cleanup is missed. This is exactly the failure mode A13 was written to prevent. Worse, cycle-5 work that touches verify.ts will see the seam as a sanctioned production pattern and propagate it. Reviewers verifying A13 by greping for setSpawnOverride miss this functionally identical seam under a different name.
recommendation: Replace _verifyOverride with the per-call seam already present in runVerification's signature: opts.spawn already accepts a VerifySpawnFn. Extend VerifyOptions with an optional `override?: VerifyOverrideFn` and have verifySpot.ts plumb it through, or have the harness inject the stub via a tool-level wrapper rather than a module-level setter. Same architecture as preflight A13.

title: verifySpot does not pass opts.spawn through to runVerification
description: verifySpot.ts calls `await runVerification(verSpec, projectRoot)` with no opts argument. The per-call opts.spawn seam in verify.ts (lines 74-85) is therefore unreachable from production tool callers — only the module-level _verifyOverride works. This forces tests to rely on the global-mutable seam (R3-003) rather than per-call injection, defeating the architectural cleanup A13 demands. It also means a future caller that wants to apply a spawn timeout (cycle 5 concern) has no API path to do so without editing verifySpot.ts.
impact: The opts.spawn parameter becomes dead production code. Any test that wants to verify spawn-error fallback (verify.ts lines 87-100) without polluting global state must use _verifyOverride, which is the wrong seam. Cycle 5's timeout work will need to plumb opts through verifySpot anyway.
recommendation: Extend runVerifySpot's input shape with an optional `opts?: VerifyOptions` parameter and forward it: `await runVerification(verSpec, projectRoot, opts)`. Default to no opts when called from MCP, but expose for the harness.
evidence:
const verSpec = (spot.verification as unknown) as VerificationSpec;

  const verResult = await runVerification(verSpec, projectRoot);

title: saveState rejection in verifySpot/selectPath/setPersonalization surfaces as unhandled MCP transport error
description: verifySpot.ts:62-63 calls `await saveState(projectRoot, advancedState)` with no try/catch. Same pattern in selectPath.ts:129 and setPersonalization.ts:104. If saveState rejects (ENOSPC, EACCES, EBUSY on Windows, transient antivirus lock), the tool function rejects with the raw fs error. The MCP server harness then turns that into the cryptic 'MCP error ...' text the green log shows at red.log:74-77 (`Unexpected token 'M', "MCP error "... is not valid JSON`). The skill cannot interpret it and cannot recover. Worse: in verifySpot the user just saw `pass: true` from runVerification but the cursor never advanced, leaving the engine in an inconsistent client-perceives-success / server-cursor-not-moved state with no signal.
impact: User-visible: cryptic transport error instead of a structured `{ pass:true, advanced:false, error: 'failed to persist cursor advance' }` response. The skill's lesson loop has no recovery path. In verifySpot's pass branch this is the worst case — verification ran (potentially against a real `pnpm build`), the user 'passed', but the next nextSpot call returns the same spot with no explanation.
recommendation: Wrap saveState in try/catch in all three tools. For verifySpot, return `{ pass: true, advanced: false, output: ..., error: 'verification passed but state persist failed: <msg>' }` so the skill can show progress without losing the failure. For selectPath/setPersonalization return `{ ok: false, errors: [...] }`.

title: validatePersonalizationValues throws on null/non-object values input
description: validatePersonalizationValues' first action is `for (const key of Object.keys(values))`. If the MCP client sends `values: null` (which is JSON-valid and crosses the wire intact), `Object.keys(null)` throws TypeError synchronously. setPersonalization.ts:82 calls this with no input guard — the typed signature `Record<string, unknown>` is a compile-time fiction across the MCP boundary. Same hazard if values is an array, a primitive, or undefined.
impact: Sending `setPersonalization({ values: null })` rejects with a synchronous TypeError that propagates as a transport error rather than the expected `{ ok: false, errors: [...] }`. Same as R3-005 the skill sees an opaque MCP error instead of a structured rejection. Trivially exploitable by a malformed client.
recommendation: At the top of setPersonalization.ts, guard with `if (typeof values !== 'object' || values === null || Array.isArray(values)) return { ok: false, errors: ['values must be an object'] };` before calling validatePersonalizationValues. Belt-and-suspenders: add the same guard inside validatePersonalizationValues so direct callers are safe.
evidence:
// Validate the submitted values
  const validationResult = validatePersonalizationValues(values, declaredOptions);

title: verify.ts compile adapter loses spawn-error context, returning bare error code as output
description: When spawnFn throws synchronously (ENOENT for the binary, EACCES on the executable, the `command.split(' ')` producing an empty/undefined cmd), the catch block returns `{ pass: false, output: e.code ?? e.message ?? String(err) }`. For ENOENT spawning `pnpm`, this yields `output: 'ENOENT'` with no mention of the command, the projectRoot, or the spawn-time context. The student sees `pass: false, output: 'ENOENT'` and cannot tell whether their build legitimately failed or whether pnpm itself is missing.
impact: Indistinguishable failure modes: a legitimate build failure (pnpm exited non-zero with stderr) and a missing-binary error both return pass:false but the latter has a one-word output. The skill's help ladder (cycle 5) cannot pick the right rung. Also undermines preflight: pnpm-available probe should have caught this, but if the user's PATH changed mid-session, verifySpot fails silently with no actionable guidance.
recommendation: Replace `output: e.code ?? e.message ?? String(err)` with `output: \`Failed to spawn '${cmd} ${args.join(' ')}' (cwd=${projectRoot}): ${e.code ?? e.message ?? String(err)}\`` so output preserves the command, cwd, and the underlying code/message. Optionally also surface a discriminated `error: 'spawn-failed'` field on VerificationResult so callers can branch.

title: verify.ts command.split(' ') breaks on quoted arguments and empty commands
description: Splitting `command` on a single space is naive. `pnpm build` works, but a path.json that declares `build_command: 'sh -c "cd app && pnpm build"'` — entirely valid — splits to args ['-c', '"cd', 'app', '&&', ...] and runs nonsense. An empty string (validation in path.ts:53 only requires it be a string, not non-empty) sets cmd=''; nodeSpawnSync then throws TypeError synchronously. Multiple consecutive spaces yield empty-string args.
impact: Cycle 4 fixtures all use 'pnpm build' so the issue is latent, but the build_command field is meant to be configurable per spec.md. Future paths declaring richer commands will silently mis-execute. The catch block in lines 87-100 catches the synchronous spawn TypeError but reports it as a build failure (pass:false) — same indistinguishability as R3-007.
recommendation: Use shell-quote parsing or split-with-quotes (e.g. `shell-quote.parse(command)`), or restrict the schema to a tokenized form `{ argv: string[] }`. At minimum, validate path.ts/phases.ts that build_command/verification.command is non-empty and tokenize defensively (filter empty results from split).

title: LoadPhasesError does not distinguish ENOENT from other read failures
description: loadPhases catches the readFile error and constructs LoadPhasesError with `e.message ?? String(err)`. The errno code (ENOENT, EACCES, EISDIR, ENOTDIR) is dropped. The contract A12 introduces a warning kind `phase-engine-phases-load-failed` that 'carries slug and reason' — but reason is just a string blob. Callers in nextSpot.ts:71-72, verifySpot.ts:37-38, selectPath.ts:111-112 stringify the error with `String(err)` and surface it to the user verbatim. There's no path for the registry-rescan recovery branch that could legitimately race against a path being deleted.
impact: When phases.json disappears between scan and use (the exact case the contract calls out: 'a path can disappear between scan and use'), the user sees an ENOENT message but the engine has no way to programmatically detect 'rescan and retry'. Same blob format hides permission errors that need a different remediation.
recommendation: Make LoadPhasesError carry a discriminated reason: `reason: 'missing' | 'permission-denied' | 'malformed-json' | 'invalid-schema'` with the original error message in a separate `details` field. Tools can branch on reason; users get clearer guidance.

title: verifySpot has no defensive handler for VerificationModeUnsupportedError
description: If a spot's verification.mode is 'test', 'simulate', or 'custom', runVerification throws VerificationModeUnsupportedError (verify.ts:103-113). verifySpot.ts does not catch this — the rejection propagates to the MCP transport and surfaces as the same opaque 'MCP error ...' text seen in red.log:74. The contract notes phase 2/3 placeholders all use mode:'compile' in cycle 4, but the schema (phases.ts:72) accepts all four modes, so a hand-edited or future fixture can produce this error path. The contract A12 even introduces a warning kind `verification-mode-unsupported` for exactly this case — but the code path that should emit it is missing.
impact: A phases.json with `mode: 'test'` (entirely schema-valid in cycle 4) crashes verifySpot via an unhandled rejection rather than returning `{ pass: false, error: 'mode test not yet supported' }`. The structured `verification-mode-unsupported` warning kind defined in warnings.ts is dead code.
recommendation: Wrap `await runVerification(verSpec, projectRoot)` in try/catch. On VerificationModeUnsupportedError, return `{ pass: false, error: 'verification-mode-unsupported: ' + err.mode }` and emit the corresponding warning. Keep generic Error rethrowing if you want crashes to surface — but this specific error is part of the contract's typed surface and should not crash the tool.
evidence:
const verSpec = (spot.verification as unknown) as VerificationSpec;

  const verResult = await runVerification(verSpec, projectRoot);
